schools.summary: The seventy(-two) appointed for the nations (Cyril; Bede), typified by Moses' seventy elders (Num 11:16) and Elim's palms; Augustine: the twelve = bishops, the seventy = presbyters; Gregory: sent two by two for the two commands of charity, going before as the Lord follows his preachers (Isa 40:3).
A widening of the mission beyond the Twelve (Matt 10:1), sent in pairs before Christ's face to ready the towns for his coming.

The words repeat those to the Twelve (Matt 9:37; John 4:35; 1 Cor 3:9); the ripe harvest, few labourers; 'send forth' (ekballē, thrust out); prayer is Christ's method for increasing the supply, for God alone qualifies and commissions (Wesley).
Cyril/Chrysostom: Christ who bids them pray the Lord of the harvest is himself that Lord, who then sends the labourers asked for.

themes: pray the Lord of the harvest
Christ the Lord of the harvest

schools.summary: 'Go' a call to courage (Cyril: like a general before battle); the lambs mark innocence (Isidore: the riotous are goats); the good Shepherd sends them not to prey but to bless (Ambrose; Isa 11:6); the wolves = Pharisees or heretics, yet the lambs convert them (Chrysostom).
Sent defenceless as lambs among wolves (Matt 10:16); their comfort and safety in the power of the Sender, not in arms.

No purse, scrip, or shoes — travel light, trusting the Sender; 'salute no man' is urgency not discourtesy (like Elisha's servant, 2 Kings 4:29), the Eastern salutation consuming much time (Matt 10:10).

Chrysostom: forbid all care for externals and distraction by greetings, for it wastes time fit for preaching; Ambrose: not that benevolence displeased, but devotedness pleased more; Lightfoot: the seventy ran before the King to the towns he would visit.

Peace the first word — 'the mother of all good things' (Chrysostom), the message and blessing the heralds bring (Ambrose); not the empty form, for it rests on the worthy or returns to the preacher (Gregory; Matt 10:12; John 14:27).

'Son of peace' a Hebraism (the 'son of' idiom) — one inclined to peace, of good report, the worthy head of the household (Isa 9:6); the peace rests on the worthy or returns in blessing to the speaker, never wasted.
Gregory: the peace rests on the house if any be predestined to life, or returns to the preacher, who is not without fruit or reward.

The herald may live by his labour, content with what is given; 'the labourer is worthy of his hire' quoted by Paul as Scripture (1 Tim 5:18; 1 Cor 9:14), suggesting Luke's Gospel was recognised by then; 'go not house to house' avoids the waste and distraction of Eastern hospitality rounds (Matt 10:10).
Gregory: he who forbade purse and scrip allows sustenance from preaching, the food being part of the labourer's wages; Theophylact: receive nourishment as a reward.

themes: the labourer worthy of his hire

schools.summary: 'Eat such things as are set before you' frees the disciples from rabbinic dietary scruples (tithing, Trumah) — gospel liberty and contentment (1 Cor 10:27; Matt 10:11); a needed lesson of common politeness for preachers (Robertson).

themes: eat what is set before you

schools.summary: Heal the sick (authenticating the mission and showing beneficence) and proclaim 'the kingdom of God is come nigh' (Matt 3:2; 10:7) — the burden of these heralds of the great King; the perfect tense marks the kingdom pressing near.
Christ provides for refusal — the rejected heralds go out into the broad streets (out of the inhospitable houses) to make their witness openly, the turning from blessing to solemn testimony.
Chrysostom: the sentence falls on us too if we refuse the guests Christ sends, as he bade them shake off the dust.

Shaking off the clinging dust (Luke 9:5; Acts 13:51) renounces all fellowship with the rejecting city (dative of disadvantage); yet the message stands — 'the kingdom is come nigh' uttered to believing and unbelieving alike, as the prophets spoke whether men heard or forbore (Ezek 2:7).

themes: the gospel a witness against the refuser

schools.summary: The same warning as to the Twelve (Matt 10:15); 'more tolerable' (anektoteron) for Sodom (Gen 19:24) than for the rejecting city — judgment in proportion to privilege; to spurn the gospel outweighs even Sodom's sin.

Ambrose: heavier punishment for rejecting the Gospel than for breaking the law; Bede: the Galilean cities worse than the Gentiles, despising the Son of God; sackcloth = remembrance of sin, ashes = death, sitting = lowliness (Jonah 3:6); Chrysostom: he mourns for our example, drawing by lament and alarm.
Tyre and Sidon would have repented at such mighty works (Matt 11:21) — the greater light, the greater guilt of the favoured cities.

The principle applied: Tyre and Sidon (the censured heathen ports, Ezek 28:21) more tolerable at the judgment than the Galilean cities (Matt 11:22) — guilt measured by light received, the despised gospel bringing the sorest doom.
Chrysostom: this sentence falls on us too — every soul that has the means of grace and neglects them stands as Chorazin did.

themes: more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon

schools.summary: Capernaum exalted (by situation, privilege, pride, and Christ's dwelling) thrust down to Hades (contrast with heaven, Isa 14:13-15; Ezek 26:20; Matt 11:23); the prophecy fulfilled in the towns' desolation; Nazareth tellingly omitted (Robertson).
Bede: cast down either for proudly resisting his preaching or beaten with more stripes for refusing the miracles that exalted her.

Encouragement and warning: to hear/despise the messengers is to hear/despise Christ and the Father who sent him (Matt 10:40; John 13:20; 5:23); 'rejecteth' (atheteō, set aside) — the grave responsibility of co-working with Christ.
Cyril/Bede: the apostles' word must be received as Christ's; all who despise the gospel despise not men only but Christ in them.
Calvin: not a mere repetition — here it is of doctrine, not offices of kindness; faith receives God in his Word.

themes: he that heareth you heareth me
faith receives God in his Word

schools.summary: Cyril: the seventy return confessing the power of him who honoured them, but rejoicing rather in miracles than in souls won (cf. Phil 4:1, Paul's 'joy and crown'); the joy good but needing to be raised higher.
'Through thy name' — the devils subject not to their own virtue but to Christ's name (Mark 16:17), the triumph their Master's.

Gregory: spoken to put down pride — the fall of the author of pride a warning; Bede: 'as lightning' a headlong fall from the high places; the disciples' victory the earnest of Satan's final conquest (Rev 12:9).

Robertson/Ellicott: the imperfect 'I was beholding' with aorist 'fall' pictures the Seventy's triumph as Satan's lightning fall; Lightfoot: Satan's power sank as the Messiah showed himself (John 12:31).
Serpents = open foes, scorpions = secret plotters (Bede, Theophylact); a literal sense not excluded (Paul's viper, Acts 28:5; Ps 91:13); the power reaches to all the enemy's force.

Ellicott: the serpents/scorpions are symbols of spiritual evil (a literal reading would make it greater than casting out demons); the kernel is 'nothing shall hurt you' — nothing separates from God's love, all works for good (Rom 16:20).

debates.question: Are the serpents and scorpions literal or symbolic?

sides.position: Chiefly symbolic of spiritual powers of evil; the kernel is final preservation
Both — a literal preservation too (Paul's viper), as sign of the spiritual

schools.summary: Rejoice not in the power over spirits but in salvation — 'names written in heaven' from the civic register, the book of life (Phil 4:3; Rev 3:5; perfect tense, enrolled); Ellicott: the OT (Exod 32:32-33; Rev 3:5) suggests the joy tempered with fear lest the name be blotted.

Christ rejoices in the Spirit over the conversion of sinners (Cyril); 'I thank thee' is thanksgiving (Bede); the 'babes' are the humble, not the dull (Bede; 1 Cor 1:27; Matt 11:25); his thanks no mark of inferiority — he calls the Father Lord of heaven and earth, against Marcion's mutilation (Epiphanius).
In the hour of the seventy's return Christ blesses the Father's sovereign good pleasure, hiding the kingdom from the self-wise and revealing it to the lowly.

themes: hid from the wise, revealed to babes

schools.summary: 'All things delivered to me' (timeless aorist, all power from the Father); the mutual knowledge of Father and Son 'an aerolite from the Johannean heaven' (Robertson; John 17:25; Matt 11:27), not to be whittled away; the Son sovereignly reveals the Father to whom he wills (John 3:35).
Cyril of Jerusalem: the angels behold as they can bear, but the Son with the Spirit knows the Father fully and reveals him as each can bear; Wesley: the Son essentially one with the Father.

themes: mutual knowledge of Father and Son

schools.summary: Christ turns from the blinded Jews to bless his disciples, to whom the Father is revealed (Theophylact); the 'seeing' is the sight of faith, not bodily eyes — many Jews saw the works yet believed not (Cyril; Matt 13:16); blessed are the eyes that see the Word's glory by faith.

themes: the privilege of the gospel age

schools.summary: Prophets and kings (Matthew: 'righteous men') desired to see what the disciples saw (1 Pet 1:10); their desire proves they knew of Christ (Chrysostom), but saw him only afar, in a glass darkly (Bede; John 8:56; Heb 11:13).
Calvin: the Church's lot now preferable to the fathers' — they had shadows, we the shining face of Christ; yet they breathed after him like hungry persons with serene faith.

themes: saw afar, in a glass darkly

schools.summary: Bede: the lawyer tempts, taking occasion from 'names written in heaven'; Cyril: he sought to entrap Christ into speaking against Moses, calling him 'Master' without being his disciple; Ambrose: skilled in the letter, ignorant of the law's spirit.
The question 'what shall I do?' is that of one trusting works (Matt 19:16; 22:35); Christ meets him on the ground of the law he professed to master.

Christ sends the lawyer to his own province ('What readest thou?', the rabbinic formula); the proper use of the law — to expose the man's need of a better righteousness, a schoolmaster to Christ (Gal 3:24; Rom 3:20).
Calvin: Christ speaks of how to be accounted righteous, in agreement with the question; the law truly shows the way of life by works, if any could fully keep it.

The lawyer joins Deut 6:5 (from the phylacteries) and Lev 19:18 (Matt 22:37); the four powers (heart, soul, strength, mind) name the whole man (Wesley); 'with all thy mind' drawn out in fuller form (Lightfoot).
Gregory of Nyssa: the clauses name the soul's faculties; Basil: 'with all' admits no division — love wasted on lower things diminishes love to God, as a vessel is emptied by what flows out.

The answer accepted as theoretically true (Ellicott); 'this do and thou shalt live' a solemn truth, not irony (Wesley) — love is life if true, tested by action; yet none can do it always, to slip once is to fail (Robertson; Lev 18:5; Rom 10:5; Gal 3:12).
Calvin: God justifies freely not because the law lacks perfect righteousness but because we fail to keep it — the impossibility shuts the self-righteous up to mercy.

themes: this do and thou shalt live
the law shuts up to mercy

schools.summary: Cyril: the question springs from pride — he thought he had no neighbour, proving his lack of love for neighbour and so for God (1 John 4:20); Ambrose: he knew not his neighbour because he believed not on Christ; Theophylact: a neighbour is defined by shared nature, not action.
'Willing to justify himself' (Luke 16:15; Rom 4:5), he sought to narrow 'neighbour' so as to appear to have kept the command — the question unmasking his heart.
The notorious robber-haunted road from Jerusalem to Jericho (Lightfoot, Barnes; Josephus on Herod's dismissed builders); the man stripped, wounded, left half dead, helpless (Matt 20:29).
Augustine: the man = Adam / the race; Jerusalem = the heavenly country fallen from; Jericho = mortality; Gill: the parable figures fallen humanity recovered by Christ (Isa 1:6).

'By chance' a coincidence that was God's test-moment (Ellicott's irony); the priest, likely returning from his temple course in priestly Jericho, was bound to mercy (Deut 22:4) yet passed by — shrinking from trouble and ceremonial defilement, or from mere want of charity (Lightfoot; Ps 38:11).

themes: passed by on the other side

schools.summary: The Levite (the only mention in the Gospels), a minister of religion likely returning from temple service, 'came and looked' more attentively than the priest yet still passed by — better in looking, worse in resisting the pity he felt; more light, more sin (Ellicott; Ps 109:25).

The hated Samaritan (John 4:9) does the neighbourly act the priest and Levite refused, 'coming where he was' in compassion; Christ boldly makes him the type of excellence after his own repulse by Samaritans (Luke 9:53; John 8:48).
Augustine/Gill: Christ himself figured in the Samaritan — 'Samaritan' = keeper, who keeps Israel and slumbers not; the despised One who had compassion on fallen humanity.

Detailed, self-denying mercy (Luke's medical precision): bound the wounds, poured oil and wine (household remedies), gave up his own beast to go on foot, brought him to an inn (pandocheion, 'receives all'); contrasted point by point with the priest and Levite.
Gill: figures Christ binding the broken-hearted and healing wounded sinners (Isa 1:6; Ps 147:3); the inn the Church where the soul is cared for till he comes.
The care continued — two denarii (two days' wages, a liberal provision) given to the host, with a carte blanche for more ('I will repay', the reliable man's word; Luke 14:14; Prov 19:17).
Gill: the departure and 'when I come again' figure Christ leaving his people in good hands to live by faith, and returning to repay all spent in their care.

Christ reverses the question — not 'whom must I love?' but 'who proved himself a neighbour?'; the Samaritan recognised a neighbour in the Jew, so the Jew must in the Samaritan; the parable disarms prejudice like Nathan's to David (2 Sam 12:7), leading the lawyer to judge against his own narrowness.

The lawyer, prejudice forbidding him to name the Samaritan, confesses 'he that shewed mercy'; 'Go and do thou likewise' commands mercy to all, friend and foe, as evidence of keeping the law (Matt 5:44; James 2:13).
Ambrose: not priesthood or knowledge of the law but compassion makes a neighbour, compassion being according to nature; Chrysostom: help any in need, Gentile or Jew, without disputing (Micah 6:8).

The parable's love now shown in deed (Bede); the village = Bethany (John 11:1); the marvel of the welcome — the creature receives the Creator, by his condescension (Augustine; Matt 25:40); Cyril: guest and host each edified and rewarded.
Martha receives Christ as an honoured guest into her house at Bethany — a true act of love, yet to be ordered aright.

To sit at the feet was the learner's place (Paul at Gamaliel's, Acts 22:3; Luke 8:35); Mary a disciple drinking in his word (imperfect, kept listening); the same character as in John 11-12 (Ellicott).
Chrysostom: at his feet, showing diligence and reverence; Augustine: her humility received the more, as waters flow to the lowest valley.

'Cumbered' = distracted, drawn about by conflicting cares; her motive good (to entertain Christ well, not covetous), her fault the fretful interruption and the improper reproof 'dost thou not care?' (1 Cor 7:35).
Augustine: Martha, troubled in her serving, interrupted the Lord and complained of her sister.

'Martha, Martha' a tender reproof (like 'Simon, Simon', Luke 22:31); 'careful' (merimnais, the 'take no thought' word of Matt 6:25) inward anxiety, 'troubled' (thorybazē) outward agitation — the divided heart robbed of peace by many cares.
Augustine: the repeated name a mark of love, or to draw her attention to listen more earnestly.

'One thing needful' = the hearing of the word / piety, not one dish (Gill rejects the low sense; Robertson notes the textual variant); Mary's 'good part' the love of God and the kingdom, which 'shall not be taken away' (John 10:28; Ps 27:4; 1 Tim 4:8; Matt 6:33).
Augustine: the many things are carnal and temporal, the one is preferred to many — for many come from one, not one from many.

themes: which shall not be taken away
